channel.title: Nature Communications
channel.description:
Nature Communications is an open access, multidisciplinary journal dedicated to publishing high-quality research in all areas of the biological, health, physical, chemical and Earth sciences. Papers published by the journal aim to represent important advances of significance to specialists within each field.

We are committed to providing an efficient service for both authors and readers. Our team of independent editors make rapid and fair publication decisions. Prompt dissemination of accepted papers to a wide readership and beyond is achieved through a programme of continuous online publication.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Glass formation in hybrid metal halides via breaking molecular rotational order
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 22 January 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-68563-5

Revealing the structural features governing ergodicity breaking is critical to understanding glass formation. Here, the authors synthesise a family of hybrid metal halide glasses, and show that the molecular shape and polarity determine rotational disorder, enabling diverse glass-forming abilities.
item.dc:title: Glass formation in hybrid metal halides via breaking molecular rotational order
item.dc:creator: Zi-Ying Li
Rui Feng
Zhi-Gang Li
Martin T. Dove
Shi-Shuang Huang
Fengxia Wei
Caijuan Shi
Ze Chang
Neng Li
Lirong Zheng
Xiaodong Li
Wei Li
Xian-He Bu

item.title: Reversible bismuth reduction-driven dark photoelectrochemistry
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 22 January 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-68359-7

Enhancing the selectivity of molecular recognition within (photo)-electrochemical systems has consistently presented a challenge. Here, the authors report a dark-enhanced photoelectrochemical mechanism for the highly selective quantitative analysis of glutathione on a BiOBr photocathode.
item.dc:title: Reversible bismuth reduction-driven dark photoelectrochemistry
item.dc:creator: Ying Qin
Yuanxing Chen
Haifei Wan
Siting Wu
Jingyi Zhang
Wenhong Yang
Yifei Chen
Liuyong Hu
Wenling Gu
Chengzhou Zhu
